description: A 'hisaro' is a health-relevant observation produced by a stated method, such as a measurement, examination, or image interpretation. The finding retains the conditions and uncertainty of that method because the same result can mean different things elsewhere. 'phamori' (symptom) is the person's experienced report; neither kind of evidence silently outranks the other or becomes a diagnosis.
articulatory_notes: Let unvoiced breath pass through the open glottis for 'h', then move into high-front unrounded 'i'. Send unvoiced air through a narrow groove along the tongue for 's' and open into stressed central 'a'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and draw the tongue back into true-mid rounded back 'o'.
usage_notes: Individual findings are countable. The observer and method show where each one came from; conditions and time locate it. Uncertainty and the original record show how far it can support a claim. 'somaki' (test) names a procedure, while 'kithero' (diagnose) draws a clinical conclusion.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A finding belongs to the method that produced it; it cannot quietly overwrite the person's report or become a diagnosis on its own.

semantic_domains.cognition: an observation whose stated method makes it available for interpretation
semantic_domains.physical: an observation concerning bodily or health condition
